Brief
EXAMPLE
Olive Oil of the Month Club
(00:01)
All right. So I want to show a brief example here. This one will be for the Olive
Oil of the Month Club. We looked at this during the mechanism component.
This offer here, the Olive Oil Hunter, this guy. And what I’m doing for this one
is just pulling stuff from the letter to populate the brief to kind of show you
what it might look like. Now I’ll do another example where I take stuff from
research and stuff from a mechanism and then pop it in as well. But one cool
thing for you as you’re trying to understand the brief and a cool exercise you
can do, is you can find sales letters that you like, and then based on that pull
stuff out. Almost copy and paste it or make notes into this brief. And it’s a
good way for you to get a feeling for the parts of a sales letter.
(00:49)
So does that make sense? So again, you can take this as you find pain points,
copy and paste them into pain points, find a paradoxical question and pop it
into here and so forth. And if you do that for several sales letters and you then
take them and apply them to a blank brief like the one we had earlier, that
can be a really cool and useful exercise. So with the Olive Oil of the Month
Club and the question of who is the audience, right? Well we kind of know it’s
foodies, it’s people who care about quality of meals. They often have guests
over to entertain, they’re motivated by prestige, uniqueness and individuality.
And then I’m gonna stop there because I wrote this stuff just based on the
sales letter really and assumptions. But then I thought, okay, well what else
can I do? What else can I know about them?
(01:33)
And I thought, well, they’re probably similar to people who would shop at
Whole Foods, right? Really there’s probably a correlation here. And again,
normally, hopefully you already know or the client knows or you know who
you’re targeting because that’s who the client wants to target. Or if you’re the

offer owner, you know who you want to target. So I just Googled Wholefoods

report, right? And it has some really interesting demographics here. Like the
key customer for the average Wholefoods location is a working parent that’s
between the age of 30 and 50. The health and fitness conscious are more
likely to shop there than somewhere else. More of you have children. And
I saw somewhere in here basically that the average, or I think it was in a
different one of these reports I found.
(02:21)
But the average customer is 156% more likely to make over 200,000 a year.
So I put typically working at least one parent in between the age of 30 and
50, more health and fitness conscious and more affluent, much higher than
the normal probability of earning over 200 K a year. What are the pain points
or biggest fears? Well really for these people, and again this is done without
doing a ton of research, but pulling from the sales letter. It’s missing out
on a luxury experience, having mediocre or less than perfect meals, having
something kept from them. Rancidness, being taken advantage of, hard to
find good quality olive oil. And long term would be always missing out is really
the key, right? That if they don’t have this olive oil or they’re always missing
out on what could be, they are always having something kept from them.
(03:06)
So for a product like this of olive oil and the way it’s positioned, is not like
long-term pain points aren’t, die of heart disease and olive oil helps. But this is
what it is. And that’s for that potential buyer. Their big thing is a FOMO or that
they’re not getting this experience that they deserve to have. That kind of stuff.
So by reversing the pain points/biggest fear is what is the one big promise
you’re going to make? How has this changed your life forever? They’re going
to see why they’ve been misled about olive oil in the past and discover the
truth about olive oil so that they experience the freshest, best tasting, most
aromatic olive oil they’ve ever had. And again, if you need to, if you didn’t look
at this yet, you can use this link, go read the letter and follow along with me.
(03:48)
What solutions for the reader’s problems exist already and why aren’t they
working? It’s basically all other olive oil brands out there and none of them
have a made on date. They’re shipped slowly, they sit around for months
before you ever buy them. The truth is that olives are fruit and they are best

get when you experience the olive oil and the product. So I put that there. So
there’s really maybe two pieces to it and that’s fine, but one is, kind of what
you get, but this is more about the experience you get. Not the product, but
the experience with the product.
(07:42)
So that’s why I would put both of these here. What is the product? Well, again,
I pulled this right from the copy, but if you were doing this and you hadn’t read
the copy yet, which is again how generally you’re going to do it. If you could
write out stuff like what’s here and then you could put it right into the sales
copy, you’d be in a great place. So Fresh Pressed Olive Oil Club is deprived
and we can write a copy about it. Long story short and I am going to make this
a little bit bigger for you too. Long story short, that’s how I founded the Fresh
Pressed Olive Oil Club. I realized if I were willing to break with the tradition
of shipping olive oil by slow cargo ship and instead rush it to America by jet,
direct from the new harvest of each olive growing country, I could be the first
to bring authentic fresh pressed oil to foodies here in America all year round.
(08:25)
I shamelessly admit that I want to make it as irresistible as I possibly can for
you to try my Fresh Pressed Olive Oil. Based on the experience of my other
customers, I know that once you try it, you’ll never want to live without it.
Think for one moment of all the things for which you will spend $1 more in
the coming months. Money which will have little or no impact on the joy and
pleasure you’ve experienced in your daily life. Contrast these with the modest
$1 I ask to help cover my shipping. This one little dollar will be your first class
ticket to a whole new world of dining pleasure and satisfaction. For just $1
you’ll get to crown about a month’s worth of your favorite dishes with the
incomparable flavor of divine, fresh-from-the-harvest olive oil. That’s dozens
of deliciously enhanced meals for just $1. And no matter what you decide, the
$39 bottle is yours to keep with my compliments.
(09:09)
Best of all, as a member of the Fresh Pressed Olive Oil Club, there is no
obligation or commitment to buy anything now or ever. Four times a year,
roughly November, February, May and August, I travel the world, stalking the
global olive groves to sample hundreds of fresh pressed olive oils right at the
peak of their harvest-fresh flavor. My trusted sources in these far away olive
groves - established over my years of globetrotting - includes hundreds of the

Is 728, like 2200 words. Basically just that and you’ve already written a third of

then there’s some little parts you’re going to see in the next section that kind

other people’s closes. So the close is like the finish, which is probably about
2000 words maybe. So say this was whatever I just said, 2,400 words, the
close is going to be maybe 1500 to 2000 words. So that’s 4,000-4,500 words.
Your leads is 500 or 1000 words. That’s 5,500 and there’s some filler stuff in
between. I mean, at that point just by doing this stuff, it’s pretty easy for you
to then finish your sales letter. So I hope that makes sense why that’s so cool.

9	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 4	Brief Example - Olive Oil
as well. And based on that, now we have the brief for olive oil.
(14:49)
So again, we did this in reverse. We pulled from a sales letter, but you can kind
of see how once you do this, and again, say I didn’t have that sales letter to go
off of, well I’ve done my research. Again, I can answer this part about who the
audience is. I know their pain points from my research. The promise is literally
just reversing the pain points. What are the solutions? I’ve got that from my
research. The mechanism I did for my mechanism part. Paradoxical question
I think about as I’m doing the brief. Metaphor I think about as I’m doing the
brief. Although I may have found some cool metaphors during my research
or paradoxical questions. Bold claim is what I’m selling, the promise of the
solution. So that’s pretty easy, right?
(15:33)
What is their pain point? What does your product do? What’s your USP of the
product? And you know that pretty fast generally. What is the product? Okay,
well we know what the product is so we’re going to write out as much detail
about that as we can. Background story, you should generally have that if it’s
your own background story or a client’s. If you don’t have it, you can get it from
the client. Generally you’re taking their words and just weaving a story. So that
part is really not that hard. And then headline ideas. If you want to, we’re going
to do a bonus module on headlines. And upsells, which you frankly can leave
blank if you have no ideas, but would be great if you do it. So, awesome.
